data.description: Completed Tauri to Electron migration. All 18 Rust backend commands ported to TypeScript IPC handlers. Frontend bridge rewritten. Build and dev server verified working. Zero Tauri imports remain in src/.
data.what_changed: Full migration from Tauri v2 to Electron 40 with electron-vite. Rust backend (~770 lines) ported to TypeScript (~500 lines across 4 IPC handler files). Frontend bridge (tauri-commands.ts) rewritten to use window.electronAPI.invoke(). File watching moved from notify-debouncer-mini to chokidar. Claude streaming moved from Tauri Channel to IPC send/on pattern.
data.files_created: electron/main.ts, electron/preload.ts, electron/ipc-handlers.ts, electron/ipc/commands.ts, electron/ipc/claude.ts, electron/ipc/watcher.ts, electron/ipc/settings.ts, electron.vite.config.ts, tsconfig.node.json
data.files_modified: src/lib/tauri-commands.ts, src/hooks/useFileWatcher.ts, src/hooks/useGitStatus.ts, src/components/ControlPanel/ProjectList.tsx, package.json, biome.json, .gitignore, index.html

data.description: Migrated Pane from Tauri v2 to Electron 40 due to WKWebView 60fps cap breaking ProMotion 120Hz support
data.why: WKWebView caps at 60fps on macOS, no workaround, Tauri closed as 'not planned'. Pane needs 120Hz ProMotion to feel like a native IDE. Electron uses Chromium which supports ProMotion natively.
data.alternatives_considered: napi-rs native modules (too complex for I/O-bound code), Rust sidecar (unnecessary overhead), staying with Tauri (dealbreaker)
data.approach: Full TypeScript port — Rust backend was only ~770 lines of I/O-bound code with no performance-critical paths

data.description: Fixed preload script not loading — Electron 40 defaults sandbox:true which blocks contextBridge in preload. Set sandbox:false in webPreferences.
data.symptom: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'on') — window.electronAPI was undefined
data.root_cause: Electron 40 defaults sandbox:true for renderer processes, which prevents preload scripts from using Node.js APIs like contextBridge
data.fix: Added sandbox: false to BrowserWindow webPreferences

data.description: electron-vite with ESM (type:module) requires .mjs output filenames. Default vite lib mode outputs .js which electron-vite can't find. Must set rollupOptions.output.entryFileNames to [name].mjs.
data.context: electron-vite dev failed with 'No electron app entry file found: out/main/main.mjs' because build produced main.js

data.description: Pane architecture changed from Tauri+Rust to Electron+TypeScript. Backend: electron/ipc/ (commands.ts, claude.ts, watcher.ts, settings.ts). Bridge: src/lib/tauri-commands.ts rewired to window.electronAPI.invoke(). Build: electron-vite replaces vite+tauri-cli.
data.files_created: electron/main.ts, electron/preload.ts, electron/ipc-handlers.ts, electron/ipc/commands.ts, electron/ipc/claude.ts, electron/ipc/watcher.ts, electron/ipc/settings.ts, electron.vite.config.ts, tsconfig.node.json
data.files_modified: src/lib/tauri-commands.ts, src/hooks/useFileWatcher.ts, src/hooks/useGitStatus.ts, src/components/ControlPanel/ProjectList.tsx, package.json, biome.json, .gitignore, index.html, src/lib/claude-types.ts

data.goal: Research Zustand v5 selector equality semantics for nested Map/Set state patterns

data.description: Deep-dived into Zustand v5 selector equality mechanics from actual source code (v5.0.11). Key findings documented in response to Aslam.
data.why: Need sub-16ms render performance with nested Map/Set state in projects store
data.source: Direct source code reading of zustand/esm/react.mjs, react-dom-client.development.js updateSyncExternalStore, zustand/esm/vanilla/shallow.mjs

data.description: Replaced per-node React components in FileTree with flat list rendering — single component renders all visible rows via useMemo'd flat array. Eliminated recursive component tree mount/unmount overhead.
data.why: One React component per tree node with 7 Zustand subscriptions each was fundamentally slow. 200 nodes = 1400 selector evaluations per store update. Flat list means one component, one subscription, useMemo to recompute visible rows.
data.alternatives: Virtualized list (react-window) — overkill for typical project trees. Canvas rendering — too complex for the benefit.

data.description: Zustand selectors returning new arrays/objects inside useSyncExternalStore cause infinite re-render loops. Must compute derived arrays via useMemo in render body, not inside selector functions. Selector must return stable references (primitives or existing object refs).
data.why: buildFlatRows() in selector returned new array every call → Object.is fails → re-render → selector runs again → new array → infinite loop

data.description: Per-project FileTree instances with display:none toggling — eliminates DOM churn on project switch
data.approach: Same pattern as Workspace.tsx Conversation components — one FileTree per project, toggled via CSS display. FileTree now takes projectId prop instead of reading activeProjectId from store. All callbacks use prop directly.

data.goal: Trace and identify the source of delay/heaviness when switching between projects in the Pane app

data.description: Identified multiple sources of delay when switching between projects in Pane
data.root_cause: 7 distinct cascading side effects fire when activeProjectId changes
data.impact: Poor UX - UI feels slow/laggy during project switch

data.description: Zustand selector granularity is the #1 performance lever in this architecture. The updateProject helper creates new project objects on every mutation, but sub-properties retain their references. Subscribing to the project object re-renders on every mutation. Subscribing to project.expandedDirs only re-renders when dirs change. This distinction is invisible to developers but huge for render performance — during streaming, FileTree was re-rendering 10-20x/sec for no reason.

data.goal: Refine Pane editor typography and visual polish

data.description: Switching Pane editor from IBM Plex Mono to Geist Mono for refined typography
data.why: Geist Mono aligns with Swiss design principles (simplicity, minimalism, precision) and Aslam's design philosophy better than generic IBM Plex Mono. Geometric without being cold, modern without trendy, from Vercel - respects craft.
data.alternatives: JetBrains Mono (functional but too common), SF Mono (system default, not distinctive), Berkeley Mono (paid)

data.description: Fixed laggy Ace editor typing by switching from controlled to uncontrolled mode
data.root_cause: Using value prop (controlled mode) caused React reconciliation on every keystroke
data.solution: Switched to defaultValue (uncontrolled mode), only update editor when file actually changes via useEffect
data.performance_gain: No React re-renders on typing, instant keystroke response

data.description: Redesigning editor syntax colors to match monochromatic design philosophy

data.philosophy: One tone family, hierarchy through weight and opacity not competing colors
data.new_approach: Use warm gray/amber tones from Pane palette, subtle opacity variations

data.description: Redesigning syntax highlighting from rainbow colors to monochromatic warm gray/amber palette
data.why: Current syntax colors (purple keywords, green strings, blue functions, yellow numbers) violate Pane's monochromatic design philosophy. User's design theory: 'One tone family used well beats five accent colors.' Hierarchy should come through weight and opacity, not competing colors.
data.approach: Use existing warm gray scale (#C8C5BE, #6D6A63, #4A4742) with subtle amber accent (#C9AE6E) for differentiation. Comments stay most subtle, keywords slightly brighter, strings/numbers use amber warmth. All within same tone family.
data.alternatives_rejected: Could have used blue accent from --pane-terminal, but that's reserved for 'machine's voice' not code syntax. Rainbow colors were default Ace theme, not intentional design.

data.goal: Redesign todo UI with expandable panel and animated status dots

data.description: Redesigning todo UI with expandable panel and animated status indicators
data.why: Current todo implementation doesn't have visual presence. User wants refined UI that shows progress clearly: expandable from top of conversation, larger dots for status, pulsing dot for active task, empty dots for pending, faint for completed.
data.approach: Create collapsible todo panel that expands downward from top of conversation area. Status dots: filled+pulsing (in_progress), filled+faint (completed), outline (pending). Use existing animation system (dotPulse keyframes already defined in globals.css).
data.user_quote: let's design the todo so it can expand from top of text area and the to do list item have slightly large dots, completed items get faint dots, current task get a pulsing dot so we know where claude is and pending get an empty dot that's not filled, that would be cool won't it
data.design_principle: Visual hierarchy through motion and opacity, not color. Monochromatic with intentional animation.

data.description: Built expandable TodoPanel with animated status dots
data.what_was_built: TodoPanel component with collapsible UI and status indicators. Expands from top of conversation area. Status dots: pulsing (in_progress), faint (completed), outline (pending). Shows progress counter and active task.
data.technical_details: 1) Created TodoPanel.tsx with StatusDot component using existing dotPulse animation. 2) Added TodoItem and TodoStatus types to projects store. 3) Added setTodos() action to projects store. 4) Integrated TodoPanel at top of Conversation component. 5) Panel shows 'progress N/M' header, expands/collapses with animated chevron. 6) Active tasks show activeForm text, completed get line-through + faint. 7) Production build successful.
data.design_decisions: Monochromatic status through opacity and motion, not color. Pulsing dot draws eye to current task. Empty outline for pending creates visual hierarchy without competing colors. Expandable panel doesn't block conversation when collapsed.

data.goal: Fix TodoPanel integration - connect it to Claude's TodoWrite tool output

data.description: TodoPanel not receiving todo data - still showing in chat instead of panel
data.issue: Built TodoPanel UI but it's not connected to Claude's actual todo system. Todos still appear in chat messages and don't populate the panel. Lost the ability to track and mark progress.
data.root_cause: TodoPanel reads from projects.todos[] but there's no integration with Claude Code's TodoWrite tool. The tool writes todos somewhere (likely through system messages) but we're not parsing them into the project state.
data.symptoms: 1) Todos show in chat as before, 2) TodoPanel stays hidden (todos.length === 0), 3) No progress tracking visible in UI
data.needs_fixing: Need to intercept TodoWrite tool calls from Claude messages and sync them to projects.todos state

data.description: Fixed TodoPanel integration - now syncs with Claude's TodoWrite tool
data.problem: TodoPanel wasn't receiving data from TodoWrite tool calls - todos stayed in chat, panel stayed empty, no progress tracking
data.solution: Added todo extraction in useClaude.ts handleClaudeMessage(). When assistant messages contain TodoWrite tool_use blocks, extract the todos array and sync to projects.setTodos(). Now TodoPanel displays live, todos disappear from chat view.
data.technical_implementation: In the 'assistant' case of message handler, loop through finalContent blocks, check for tool_use type with name='TodoWrite', extract input.todos, call store.setTodos(projectId, todos). TodoPanel reads from project.todos via Zustand selector, updates automatically.
data.result: TodoPanel now shows live progress with pulsing dots for active tasks, faint dots for completed, outline for pending. Progress counter updates. Todos no longer clutter chat.

data.goal: Debug why TodoPanel integration isn't working
Make TodoPanel intercept and render Claude Code's TodoWrite data with custom UI

data.description: Connected TodoPanel to Claude Code's TodoWrite with custom rendering
data.what_was_done: 1) Intercept TodoWrite tool calls in useClaude.ts and sync to project.todos state. 2) Filter out TodoWrite from MessageBubble so it doesn't show in chat. 3) TodoPanel reads from project.todos and renders with custom UI (pulsing dots, faint completed, outline pending). 4) Added console logging for debugging.
data.technical_implementation: In useClaude handleClaudeMessage(), loop through assistant message content blocks, extract TodoWrite tool_use blocks, call store.setTodos(). In MessageBubble, filter out TodoWrite from tool blocks so they don't render via ToolActivity. TodoPanel subscribes to project.todos via Zustand and displays with animated status dots.
data.result: Now TodoWrite data flows from Claude Code → useClaude → projects.todos → TodoPanel. Todos appear in expandable panel at top, not in chat clutter. Has console logs to verify data flow.

data.goal: Redesign TodoPanel to integrate with InputBar and expand upward

data.description: Redesign TodoPanel to integrate with InputBar instead of conversation top
data.why: User wants TodoPanel to be part of the textarea UI, not a separate section at conversation top. Should be hidden by default, expand upward from InputBar when toggled. Add 'todo' indicator next to the 3-dot menu that shows when todos exist.
data.current_implementation: TodoPanel sits at top of Conversation component, always visible when todos exist
data.new_design: 1) Move TodoPanel to integrate with InputBar, 2) Add 'todo' button next to 3-dot menu in InputBar, 3) Panel expands upward (not downward) when clicked, 4) Hidden by default even when todos exist
data.user_quote: it should by default be hidden until i want to expand it then it expands up, actually i'm thinking if there's a todo, maybe we should add alongside the 3 dots indicator the word to do and when you click on that, the panel expands up

data.goal: Recover from accidental project deletion and fix the deletion bug

data.description: File deletion bug triggered full project deletion despite fix being implemented
data.context: User attempted to delete single file, entire Pane project folder was deleted. Notes show deletion bug was supposedly fixed on 2026-02-22 to use Trash via AppleScript instead of permanent deletion. Either fix didn't work or there's another deletion code path.
data.impact: Complete project source code loss, only release bundle survived

data.description: Found the deletion bug: delete_file handler uses fs.promises.rm with recursive:true
data.why: The bundled code still has permanent deletion. Notes claim it was fixed to use AppleScript + Trash but the built bundle doesn't reflect that fix. Either the fix wasn't built into the release, or there are two deletion code paths.
data.fix_needed: Replace with AppleScript-based Trash move for safe, recoverable deletion

data.description: Will modify the recovered JavaScript directly to fix the deletion bug
data.why: No TypeScript source files exist. The bundled .mjs file is the only source we have. We'll fix it directly, then rebuild the app.
data.alternative_considered: Reconstruct full TypeScript project - rejected because time-consuming and we can fix the critical bug faster by patching the .mjs directly

data.description: Fixed deletion bug by replacing fs.rm with AppleScript Trash move
data.solution: Modified delete_file handler to use osascript with Finder's delete command, which moves files to Trash instead of permanent deletion. Throws error if Trash move fails instead of falling back to permanent deletion.
data.rebuilt: Packaged fixed code into new app.asar and replaced in Pane.app bundle

data.goal: Recovering lost development session from notes.md — 10 fixes applied, then discovered and fixed critical Zustand Map reference instability causing full-tree re-renders on every store update

data.description: Complete recovery of lost development session — 10 fixes from notes.md implemented
data.fixes: 1. Claude streaming channel mismatch (CRITICAL - was completely broken), 2. Competing scroll effects, 3. Right-click select before context menu, 4. Separate editor font size with persistence, 5. Remove chat border from assistant messages, 6. Remove highlightActiveLine + ::selection CSS, 7. Filter TodoWrite from chat, 8. Move TodoPanel into InputBar, 9. Pure theme, 10. Remove dead code (useCodeMirror.ts, transition-colors)

data.description: Critical Claude streaming channel mismatch — backend sends to claude-stream:${projectId} but frontend listened on claude_event_${projectId}. Streaming was completely broken.
data.fix: Changed channel name to match backend + switched from finally{} cleanup to self-cleaning listener pattern that waits for processEnded/error event

data.description: Fixed channel mismatch + self-cleanup pattern for Claude streaming
data.pattern: Self-cleaning IPC listener — stays active until processEnded/error, then removes itself via setTimeout(() => cleanup?.(), 0). No finally{} block that could remove listener before processEnded arrives.

data.description: Zustand Map reference instability causing full-tree re-renders on every store update. App was stuttering badly — unusable during Claude streaming.
data.root_cause: updateProject() creates new Map(state.projects) on every call. 5 components/hooks subscribed to s.projects (the whole Map): App.tsx, Workspace.tsx, ProjectList.tsx, useFileWatcher.ts, useGitStatus.ts. During streaming, 50+ text deltas/sec each created a new Map, triggering full tree re-render cascade.

data.description: Fixed Zustand Map re-render cascade by eliminating all direct s.projects subscriptions
data.fixes: App.tsx: removed s.projects subscription, use getState() in effect. Workspace.tsx: derive stable primitives (activeMode, activeRoot) via selectors. useFileWatcher/useGitStatus: removed s.projects from deps, use getState() inside effects. ProjectList.tsx: useShallow to derive stable array of {id,name,hasUnread}.
data.pattern: NEVER subscribe to s.projects directly. Always derive stable primitives in selectors, or use getState() for imperative reads inside effects/callbacks.

data.description: Zustand + Map stores: the || [] inside selectors creates infinite render loops. Always use a module-level EMPTY constant (e.g. const EMPTY_TODOS: Todo[] = []) as fallback instead of inline [] or {} in selectors.
data.context: InputBar and TodoPanel both had useProjectsStore(s => s.projects.get(id)?.conversation.todos || []) which created a new array reference on every render when todos was undefined, causing Maximum update depth exceeded error.
data.fix: const EMPTY_TODOS: Todo[] = []; then use ?? EMPTY_TODOS inside selector

data.description: Added Pure theme as third theme option (Ink/Paper/Pure) — white #FFFFFF background, high contrast monochromatic syntax
data.why: Notes.md described it as part of the original build. Three themes now cycle: system -> dark -> light -> pure -> system. Pure is maximum contrast — white canvas, black ink, deeper gray keywords, stronger amber strings.
data.files: globals.css [data-theme=pure], workspace.ts Theme type + cycle, Settings.tsx theme buttons

data.description: Fixed "Maximum update depth exceeded" infinite render loop caused by 9 Zustand selectors returning non-primitive values (full Project objects, conversation objects, Maps, arrays) from the projects store. During Claude streaming, appendToLastAssistantText fires 50+ times/sec, each creating a new Map via updateProject(). Components subscribing to s.projects.get(id) got a new object reference on every update, causing cascading re-renders that exceeded React's update depth limit.
data.fix: Replaced all 9 selectors that returned full Project objects with targeted primitive selectors. For non-primitive values that components actually need (arrays like messages, todos, dirContents entries), wrapped with useShallow from zustand/react/shallow. For computed values like dirHasChanges, moved computation inside the selector to return a boolean instead of the Map.
data.affected_files: Conversation.tsx, FileTree.tsx (2 instances), ControlPanel.tsx, FileViewer.tsx, FuzzyFinder.tsx, FileSearch.tsx, InputBar.tsx, TodoPanel.tsx
data.root_cause: updateProject() creates { ...project, ...updates } spread on every call, so even though the Map.get() returns the same logical project, it's a new object reference every time. Zustand uses Object.is comparison, so every subscriber to .get() re-renders on every store update.
data.pattern: NEVER subscribe to s.projects.get(id) in Zustand selectors — always extract the specific primitive value you need inside the selector. For arrays/objects, use useShallow.

data.description: Zustand + Map store pattern: The combination of Map-based state and spread-based updates means EVERY selector that returns .get() output will fire on EVERY store update, not just updates to the key you're interested in. This is because updateProject creates a new Map AND a new project object via spread on every call. The only safe patterns are: (1) return primitives from selectors, (2) use useShallow for arrays/objects you genuinely need, (3) use getState() for imperative reads in event handlers/effects.
data.applies_to: Any Zustand store using Map<string, object> with spread-based updates

data.description: Fixed "Maximum update depth exceeded" infinite loop in FileTree.tsx. The auto-load effect used `!hasChildren` (children.length > 0) as a guard, but empty directories always have hasChildren=false even after loading. This caused: loadDir → sets empty array → hasChildren still false → isLoading false → effect re-fires → loadDir again → infinite loop. Fix: added `isDirLoaded` selector checking `dirContents.has(entry.path)` which returns true even for empty directories.
data.root_cause: Empty directory infinite loop in FileTreeNode useEffect

data.description: Comprehensive audit of "Maximum update depth exceeded" in Pane. Found 3 root causes: (1) Empty directory infinite loop — useEffect guard used `!hasChildren` (children.length > 0) but empty dirs always have 0 children, causing loadDir to fire infinitely. Fixed with `isDirLoaded` (dirContents.has()). (2) React hooks ordering violation — isDirLoaded hook and useEffect were placed after `if (!activeProjectId) return null` conditional return in FileTreeNode. Moved all hooks before the early return. (3) Module-level `settingsLoaded` flag wasn't reset on cleanup, meaning after error boundary recovery the save subscription would immediately start saving empty store state. Added `settingsLoaded = false` to cleanup.
data.audit_scope: Read every single component, hook, and store file in the renderer
data.safe_patterns_confirmed: All useEffect deps use primitives or useShallow; all store mutations are in callbacks/effects not render; no circular subscriptions; useGitStatus throttles properly; conversation save subscription only fires on length/session/processing changes not streaming deltas

data.goal: Research "Maximum update depth exceeded" errors in React 18 with Zustand v5, StrictMode, and Vite HMR

data.description: Comprehensive research on "Maximum update depth exceeded" errors with Zustand v5 + React 18 StrictMode + Vite HMR completed. Multiple distinct root causes identified.
data.causes: 1) Zustand v5 selector reference instability (new objects/arrays each render), 2) useSyncExternalStore getSnapshot caching requirement, 3) Module-level let variables persisting across HMR but resetting on full reload, 4) StrictMode double-mount causing useEffect to run twice and interact with module-level flags, 5) HMR re-executing store modules creating new instances
data.solutions: useShallow for object/array selectors, import.meta.hot.data for HMR state preservation, avoid module-level mutable state, use refs or React state instead

data.description: Removed React.StrictMode and added HMR protection for Zustand stores to fix persistent 'Maximum update depth exceeded' error during development
data.why: StrictMode double-mounts effects which interacts badly with module-level flags (settingsLoaded) and Zustand v5's useSyncExternalStore. HMR recreates store instances causing stale subscriptions. Both patterns are documented failure modes for Zustand v5 + React 18 + Vite.
data.alternatives: Could have kept StrictMode and used import.meta.hot.data for all module-level variables, but StrictMode provides minimal value for a production Electron app
data.hmr_pattern: Wrap create() in factory function, store instance in import.meta.hot.data.__STORE_NAME__

data.description: Found and fixed the ACTUAL root cause of 'Maximum update depth exceeded'. ProjectList.tsx used useShallow with a selector that created inline objects: s.projectOrder.map(id => ({ id, name: p.name, hasUnread: p.hasUnreadCompletion })). useShallow compares array elements with Object.is, but new object literals always fail Object.is — so getSnapshot always returns 'changed', causing useSyncExternalStore to loop infinitely. Fix: extracted each row into a ProjectRow component with individual primitive selectors (name: string, hasUnread: boolean, isActive: boolean).
data.root_cause: useShallow + inline objects in selector → Object.is always false → infinite useSyncExternalStore loop
data.pattern: NEVER create objects inside useShallow selectors. useShallow only stabilizes arrays/objects of primitives, not arrays of objects.
data.stack_trace: at ProjectList (ProjectList.tsx:25:41) → at ControlPanel → at App → at ErrorBoundary

data.description: Fixed chat scroll stuttering caused by three performance issues in Conversation.tsx
data.fix1: Removed useShallow on messages selector — was doing expensive element-by-element comparison on every store update, and always returning a new reference during streaming
data.fix2: Changed toolResultMap useMemo dependency from messages array to messageCount — tool results only arrive as new messages, not mutations, so no need to recompute on streaming text deltas
data.fix3: Replaced messages-dependent auto-scroll useEffect with RAF loop during processing — decouples scroll from React render cycle, eliminating jank
data.fix4: Wrapped MessageBubble in React.memo — prevents re-rendering unchanged messages when toolResultMap reference changes

data.description: useShallow on arrays of objects is almost always wrong for streaming data. During Claude streaming, every text delta creates a new messages array AND mutates the last message object. useShallow does O(n) comparison on every store update, which is worse than just subscribing to the array reference directly. For streaming scenarios: (1) subscribe to array reference directly, (2) memo individual list items, (3) use message count as dependency for derived data that only changes when messages are added/removed.

data.goal: Fix scroll performance to achieve true 120Hz ProMotion smoothness in chat

data.description: Chat scrolling still feels laggy despite React render optimizations. The whole reason for migrating from Tauri (WKWebView capped at 60fps) to Electron was 120Hz ProMotion support. Need to investigate if the problem is CSS/layout thrashing, Electron compositor settings, or something else beyond React render cycles.

data.goal: Research Electron scroll performance, ProMotion support, and optimization techniques

data.description: Comprehensive research on Electron scroll performance, ProMotion 120Hz support, CSS containment, virtualization, and passive listeners for Pane IDE
data.key_findings: Chromium auto-supports 120Hz on ProMotion, GPU rasterization needs force flag on some Macs, content-visibility: auto is CSS-native virtualization, passive listeners default on document-level in Chrome 73+, @tanstack/virtual is best for chat virtualization

data.description: Fixed scroll performance at three layers: GPU compositor, CSS containment, and passive listeners. The previous session only addressed React render cycles which was the wrong layer entirely.
data.layer1_gpu: Added enable-gpu-rasterization, enable-zero-copy, ignore-gpu-blocklist, enable-smooth-scrolling to Electron main process. Without these, Electron uses software rasterization on Retina — CPU compositing millions of pixels instead of GPU.
data.layer2_css: Added content-visibility:auto + contain-intrinsic-size:auto 80px to every message bubble. Browser now skips layout/paint/render for off-screen messages entirely during scroll — native CSS virtualization without any library.
data.layer3_passive: Added {passive:true} to scroll event listener. Browser can start compositing next scroll frame without waiting for JS handler to return.
data.layer4_animation: Only apply animate-fadeSlideUp to newly mounted messages via useRef flag. Old messages scrolled into view no longer trigger animation evaluation.
data.why: The whole Tauri-to-Electron migration was for 120Hz ProMotion. Software rasterization on a 3024x1964 display is fundamentally incompatible with that goal.

data.description: Scroll performance on high-DPI displays is a compositor problem, not a React problem. On a 3024x1964 ProMotion display, Electron defaults to software rasterization because Chromium GPU-blocklists many Mac GPUs. This means the CPU is compositing ~6 million pixels per frame. No amount of React.memo or useShallow optimization will fix that. The fix hierarchy: (1) GPU rasterization flags, (2) CSS content-visibility for native virtualization, (3) passive scroll listeners, (4) React render optimization is last resort.

Scroll jank in both chat and editor traced to custom ::-webkit-scrollbar CSS which disables macOS native overlay scrollbar GPU-accelerated compositing. Replaced with `scrollbar-width: thin; scrollbar-color` (standard CSS). Also added `will-change: transform` on all scroll containers (Conversation, Terminal, FileTree, Ace scroller) to promote them to GPU compositor layers for async scrolling.
data.root_cause: Custom ::-webkit-scrollbar forces Chromium to render software scrollbar instead of macOS native overlay scrollbar
data.fix: scrollbar-width: thin + scrollbar-color + will-change: transform on scroll containers
data.lesson: Never use ::-webkit-scrollbar on macOS if you want GPU-accelerated scrolling

data.description: Root cause of 20fps scroll jank: Electron 33 has a bug on macOS 26 Tahoe where a private _cornerMask API override in ElectronNSWindow forces WindowServer to treat window masks as dynamic, causing persistent high GPU load. Fixed in electron/electron#48376, backported to Electron 36+. Upgraded Pane from Electron 33.4.11 to 40.6.0 (Chromium 144).
data.root_cause: ElectronNSWindow._cornerMask override makes WindowServer rerender masks per-window per-frame on macOS 26
data.lesson: Always check Electron version compatibility with current macOS version

data.goal: Research Zed editor rendering architecture and Electron scroll performance optimization for 120Hz ProMotion

data.description: Deep research into Zed editor rendering architecture and Chromium scroll performance for Pane IDE optimization
data.why: Pane migrated from Tauri WKWebView (60fps cap) to Electron. Need to maximize scroll performance on 120Hz ProMotion. Research covers: Zed's GPUI Metal renderer, Chromium compositor thread architecture, specific CSS/JS patterns that cause scroll jank, and proven Electron optimizations.
data.key_findings: 1) Zed uses custom GPUI framework with Metal shaders, SDF rendering, texture atlases - fundamentally different approach than web. 2) Chromium has compositor thread that handles scroll independently of main thread - but scroll falls to main thread when non-passive event listeners or non-fast-scrollable regions exist. 3) Key Electron flags: disable-frame-rate-limit, disable-gpu-vsync. 4) CSS: content-visibility:auto, will-change:transform for layer promotion, passive event listeners. 5) Virtual scrolling with TanStack Virtual for large lists. 6) Zed discovered macOS ProMotion downclocks refresh rate when frames aren't consistently presented - they use CADisplayLink to maintain 120Hz.

data.goal: Research scroll momentum settling stutter in Electron on macOS ProMotion 120Hz displays

data.description: Research on scroll momentum settling stutter in Electron on macOS ProMotion 120Hz displays revealed multiple root causes: 1) macOS ProMotion VRR drops from 120Hz to 60Hz during momentum scroll deceleration phase as Apple's adaptive refresh rate conserves power when input velocity decreases, 2) Chromium's CVDisplayLink doesn't properly handle VRR transitions causing frame pacing hitches (CADisplayLink fix in progress), 3) Electron's _cornerMask override caused WindowServer GPU spikes on macOS 26 (fixed in Electron 38.2.0+), 4) CSS layout patterns with nested flex + min-height: 0 can create non-fast scrollable regions forcing main-thread scroll fallback
data.context: User reports smooth scroll initiation but 2-3 stutters during last 300ms of momentum settling. FPS counter shows 117-120fps throughout. Electron 40.6.0 on macOS 26.
data.key_finding: The stutters are likely caused by macOS ProMotion VRR dropping refresh rate during momentum deceleration phase, combined with Chromium frame pacing not using targetTimestamp for rate transitions

data.description: Electron scroll performance on macOS ProMotion 120Hz: the remaining momentum settling stutter is a known Chromium bug (Issue 40202100) — CVDisplayLink doesn't handle VRR refresh rate tier transitions cleanly during scroll deceleration. This is NOT fixable at the app level. Key fixes that DID help: (1) Remove continuous RAF loops (FPS counter was worst offender), (2) Replace Tailwind animate-pulse with compositor-isolated animation (will-change: opacity + contain: strict), (3) Add overscroll-behavior: contain to scroll containers, (4) Flatten flex nesting depth to reduce non-fast scrollable regions, (5) Remove all custom scrollbar CSS, (6) Remove will-change: transform from scroll containers. Flags that made things WORSE: disable-frame-rate-limit and disable-gpu-vsync dropped FPS to 50.
data.context: Multi-session investigation of scroll jank on macOS 26 Tahoe with ProMotion 120Hz display
data.key_finding: Chromium CVDisplayLink vs CADisplayLink is the root cause of momentum settling stutter — upstream fix needed

data.description: Upgraded Electron from 33.4.11 to 40.6.0 to fix macOS 26 Tahoe WindowServer GPU bug (electron/electron#48311). This was the single biggest performance improvement — went from 20fps feel to near-native.
data.why: Electron 33 had _cornerMask override that forced WindowServer to re-render masks per-window per-frame on macOS 26
data.alternatives: Could have patched Electron 33 directly but upgrade to 40 gets all fixes

data.summary: Resolved scroll performance investigation. Reverted problematic Chromium flags (disable-frame-rate-limit, disable-gpu-vsync) that were dropping FPS to 50. Applied final round of performance fixes: removed FPS counter RAF loop, replaced animate-pulse with compositor-isolated pane-pulse animation, added overscroll-behavior: contain to scroll containers, flattened Workspace flex nesting from 6 to 4 levels. Committed full Electron migration + feature recovery + performance work and pushed to remote.
pendingWork: Remaining scroll momentum settling stutter is a known Chromium CVDisplayLink bug (Issue 40202100) — not fixable at app level, waiting on upstream CADisplayLink migration
Consider monitoring Chromium/Electron releases for CADisplayLink fix
useCodeMirror.ts was deleted (dead code from Ace migration)

data.goal: Thorough comparison between old working Pane build and current source to recover lost work

data.description: Completed thorough comparison between old working Pane build (/tmp/pane-old/) and current source. Identified 12 critical differences including lost ToolActivity expanded views, missing 3-dot animation, theme naming changes, missing deep directory preloading, and FileViewer Ace configuration gaps.
data.lost_components: ExpandedEditInput, ExpandedWriteInput, ExpandedTodoInput, StatusDot, shortenPath, buildFlatRows

data.goal: Continue implementing the recovery plan - fix remaining differences between old Pane build and current source

data.description: Completed all recovery plan items. Fixed ToolActivity key (index→toolBlock.id), added Escape handler to Settings, added editor font size control to Settings, verified all 10 plan fixes + dead code cleanup are complete.

data.goal: Address all missing pieces from the missing pieces document

data.description: Plan mode UI: Track plan mode state via detecting ExitPlanMode tool calls in stream. When ExitPlanMode is detected, pause processing and show accept/reject buttons in InputBar. Accept sends approval message, reject sends rejection message to Claude.
data.why: User wants to accept or reject Claude's plans before execution, like Claude Code's plan mode. The InputBar processing area is the natural place since there are no todos during plan mode.
data.alternatives: Could have used a modal dialog but that's not Pane's style — keep it inline and minimal

data.description: Opacity stacking kills contrast: when a secondary color (already ~40% contrast) gets a /40 or /50 Tailwind opacity modifier, the effective contrast drops to ~16-20%. Functional UI elements (buttons, labels, tool names) need at least /60-/70 on secondary colors. Only ambient, non-functional elements (placeholders, decorative dots) can go below /50. The design philosophy says hierarchy through weight and size, not invisibility.
data.fix: Bumped toolbar icons /40→/60, tool activity text /50→/70, tool labels opacity-50→opacity-70, status dots 25%→40%, copy icon /30→/50, cost text /50→/60, esc buttons /40→/50, todo button /50→/70, reject button /50→/70

data.goal: Design implementation plan for editor word wrap, git commit textarea, git panel redesign, and responsive width fixes

data.description: Completed 4-fix plan: editor word wrap, responsive modals, FileTree/Git mutual exclusion, GitStatus full redesign
data.fix1: Added wrap: true to AceEditor setOptions in FileViewer.tsx
data.fix2: FileSearch w-[640px] → w-full max-w-[640px] mx-4, FuzzyFinder w-[560px] → w-full max-w-[560px] mx-4
data.fix3: ControlPanel: gitExpanded→gitPanelActive, FileTree/GitStatus mutually exclusive, auto-close on project change
data.fix4: GitStatus rewrite: no tabs, unified changes+history scroll, auto-growing textarea with Cmd+Enter commit, branch+pull/push header, contrast fixes on CommitRow

data.goal: Design search experience for FuzzyFinder and FileSearch that matches Pane's distinctive design language

data.description: Search redesign: dropped card chrome (bg-pane-surface + border) from both FuzzyFinder and FileSearch. Input lives in a borderless rounded pill, results materialize against the dark overlay. Selected rows glow with bg-pane-surface against transparent backdrop.
data.why: User said search looked like every other IDE's generic command palette. Pane's language is opacity-driven, borderless, generous spacing. The card was the main offender — boxing everything in a bordered surface is the VS Code pattern.
data.alternatives: Could have kept the card and just changed styling inside, but dropping the card entirely creates a more distinctive feel — the input pill floats alone, results appear below it against the overlay void.

data.goal: Design polish session: editor fixes, git panel redesign, search redesign, InputBar border removal

data.description: Editor word wrap: added wrap: true to AceEditor setOptions
data.why: User said horizontal scrolling to find end of text was torture. Single line fix in FileViewer.tsx setOptions.

data.description: Editor scrollPastEnd: 0.5 — allows scrolling past EOF so last line can sit at top of viewport instead of stuck at bottom
data.why: User complained that typing starts from the bottom when at end of file. Ace with height=100% fills viewport but content ends wherever it ends. scrollPastEnd: 0.5 adds half-viewport of empty space after last line so you can scroll it up.
data.first_attempt: scrollPastEnd: 0 was wrong — that disables scroll past end entirely, making the problem worse

data.description: Removed all input borders across the app: InputBar chat textarea, git commit textarea. Pane inputs are borderless — defined by bg-pane-surface container, not lines.
data.why: User explicitly hates borders on input areas. This is now a firm design rule: no border on any textarea or input in Pane.
data.files: InputBar.tsx (removed border border-pane-border from wrapper div), GitStatus.tsx (bg-transparent, no border on commit textarea)

data.description: Git panel redesign: removed tabs (changes/history), replaced with unified scrollable view. GitStatus now takes over full panel area (mutual exclusion with FileTree), not a cramped 300px overlay.
data.why: User said tabs were unnecessary complexity and git should cover the whole panel area. ControlPanel now toggles between FileTree and GitStatus. GitStatus has: branch+pull/push header, scrollable changes+history sections with tiny uppercase labels, auto-growing textarea with Cmd+Enter to commit.
data.gitPanelActive: Local useState, not Zustand — avoids Map reference instability bug
data.auto_close: useEffect closes git panel on project change or non-git repo

data.description: Git commit input: replaced single-line input with auto-growing textarea. Cmd+Enter to commit, Enter adds newlines naturally. Auto-grows up to 120px (~5 lines).
data.why: User called the single-line commit input a design violation — almost zero height, doesn't grow, just scrolls. Same auto-grow pattern as InputBar: reset height to auto, set to Math.min(scrollHeight, 120).
data.hint: cmd+enter to commit hint appears contextually when there's text and uncommitted changes

data.description: Search redesign: dropped overlay backdrop, start collapsed (input pill only), results expand on type. No pre-loaded file list.
data.why: User said search looked like every generic IDE command palette. Three iterations: 1) Dropped card chrome (separate input pill + results block) — user said transparent results were wrong. 2) Merged into one bg-pane-surface block — user said why separate input from results, make it expand. 3) Dropped overlay entirely, start collapsed, results only on type.
data.fuzzy_finder: Empty query returns no results (was returning top 50 files). Pill sits alone until you type.
data.file_search: grep label in terminal accent color, pattern placeholder, dotPulse loading animation, line numbers in terminal accent
data.key_insight: The overlay itself was the most generic thing — Spotlight-style floating panel with no backdrop is more Pane

data.description: Pane design rule: no borders on inputs, ever. Inputs are defined by their container surface (bg-pane-surface), not by border lines. User explicitly rejected borders on chat textarea, git commit textarea, and search input areas across three separate conversations.
data.pattern: Container defines the input, not the input defining itself. bg-pane-surface rounded-lg on the wrapper, bg-transparent on the actual input/textarea.

data.description: Pane design rule: no overlay backdrop on floating UI. Search panels float over content like Spotlight \u2014 no darkened overlay. The overlay was the most generic thing about the old search design.
data.context: User pushed back on search design three times, each time stripping away more generic patterns: first the card border, then the separate input/results blocks, finally the overlay itself.

data.description: Pane design rule: search should start collapsed and expand on demand. Don't pre-load results. FuzzyFinder was showing top 50 files on open \u2014 user said that's loading the panel with everything at once. Show nothing until the user types.
data.applies_to: Any future search or filter UI in Pane — start minimal, expand with content

data.description: Swapped mono font from IBM Plex Mono to Geist Mono Variable. Used @fontsource-variable/geist-mono (not the Next.js-only `geist` package). Removed @fontsource/ibm-plex-mono.
data.why: User requested Geist Mono. The `geist` npm package has strict exports map blocking direct woff2 access from CSS @font-face. @fontsource-variable/geist-mono works out of the box with Vite.
data.pattern: Always use @fontsource-variable packages for non-Next.js font imports — the official packages (like `geist`) are Next.js-only

data.description: First distributable Pane build — Pane-0.1.0-arm64.dmg (119MB) and x64 (125MB). Ad-hoc signed, no notarization yet.

data.goal: Design a customizable keyboard shortcut rebinding system for Pane
Understand the full pipeline of Claude response rendering: content block types, streaming flow, and component hierarchy

data.description: Complete pipeline of Claude response rendering in Pane: Identified exact content block types handled, streaming flow, component hierarchy, and missing content types.
data.context: Thorough code review of Claude response rendering pipeline across all key files
data.key_findings: Pane currently handles only 3 content block types (text, tool_use, tool_result) but Claude API supports at least 4. Only 2 stream event types handled (content_block_delta with text_delta, content_block_start for tool_use). No handling for content_block_stop or other block start types.
data.impact: May be silently dropping tool_result blocks, text deltas during tool use, or other stream events. This could result in incomplete tool results or missing content blocks in conversations.

data.goal: Understand stream-json format handling: thinking blocks, server_tool_use, web_search results. Map exact signatures and line numbers across 7 critical files.
Understand message flow from projects store to conversation rendering UI, including ProjectState/ConversationState shapes and message update methods

data.description: Deep analysis of Claude Code stream-json format handling architecture. Read and mapped all 7 critical files: useClaude.ts, claude-types.ts, MessageBubble.tsx, ToolActivity.tsx, MarkdownText.tsx, projects.ts, and main.mjs. Documented stream event types, content block structures, and rendering pipelines.

data.goal: Design implementation plan for improving Pane's Claude output rendering: thinking blocks, markdown improvements, server tools, token usage

data.description: Completed Claude output rendering improvements: thinking blocks, server tool use (web search), markdown links/blockquotes/tables, token usage display, and --include-partial-messages CLI flag
data.files_modified: main.mjs, claude-types.ts, MarkdownText.tsx, projects.ts, useClaude.ts, MessageBubble.tsx, ToolActivity.tsx
data.scope: Full pipeline: types → store → stream processing → UI rendering

data.goal: Thoroughly explore how input_json_delta streaming works in Claude API and how to integrate it into Pane's architecture
Thoroughly explore Claude's planning mode implementation in Pane and design proper support for EnterPlanMode/ExitPlanMode
Design implementation plan for streaming tool inputs (input_json_delta) and proper planning mode (EnterPlanMode)

data.description: Implemented streaming tool inputs (input_json_delta) and proper planning mode (EnterPlanMode/ExitPlanMode) support
data.streaming_inputs: Module-level pendingToolJson accumulator, reset on each tool_use content_block_start, parses incrementally via JSON.parse try/catch, updates store on each successful parse
data.planning_mode: isPlanning state flag set on EnterPlanMode, cleared on ExitPlanMode/processEnded/error/abort. InputBar shows 'planning' label. Reject no longer auto-sends — lets user type feedback.
data.todowrite_fix: Moved TodoWrite detection from content_block_start (where input is {}) to input_json_delta handler (where input is actually parsed)

data.description: Fixed stuck state when Claude process hangs after sending result message. Added 5-second safety timeout in renderer that force-clears isProcessing if processEnded never arrives. Also added SIGKILL fallback after 3s SIGTERM in abort handler.
data.root_cause: Claude CLI process hangs after sending result message but before exiting. processEnded event never fires because child.on('close') never triggers. isProcessing stays true, UI is frozen.
data.fix: Safety timer starts when result message is received. If processEnded doesn't arrive within 5s, finishProcessing() is called directly. Abort also sends SIGKILL after 3s if SIGTERM doesn't work.

data.goal: Understand what permission-mode plan does

data.description: Text-based plan detection instead of EnterPlanMode/ExitPlanMode tool blocks
data.why: With --dangerously-skip-permissions, Claude CLI bypasses the entire permission system including plan mode tools. EnterPlanMode/ExitPlanMode are never emitted. Our --append-system-prompt already tells Claude to present plans as text ending with 'Ready to proceed — send go to start'. Detecting this pattern in the result handler is more reliable than expecting tool blocks that won't come.
data.alternatives: Considered switching to --permission-mode plan but that prevents ALL tool execution. Considered --permission-mode default with canUseTool but that requires --input-format stream-json for bidirectional communication which adds complexity.

data.description: --dangerously-skip-permissions disables ALL permission-related tool calls including EnterPlanMode/ExitPlanMode
data.context: EnterPlanMode and ExitPlanMode are part of Claude CLI's permission system. When using --dangerously-skip-permissions, the entire permission evaluation flow is skipped, so these tools are never called. The plan mode flag --permission-mode plan is different — it makes Claude read-only (can't execute any tools). For Pane's use case where we want Claude to freely use tools AND present plans, text-based detection via --append-system-prompt is the correct approach.

data.goal: Understand terminal feature implementation: UI components, backend execution, state management, shell persistence, and cwd tracking

data.description: Explored and documented terminal feature implementation in Pane app
data.findings: Found complete terminal implementation: UI in Terminal.tsx, backend in main.mjs, state in projects store, IPC bridge in tauri-commands.ts

data.description: Terminal cwd tracking via pwd marker appended to every command
data.why: Each terminal command spawns a fresh process, so cd has no effect across commands. By appending echo ___PANE_PWD___$(pwd) to every command, we capture the resulting cwd after execution. The marker is stripped from visible output and used to update the cwd state for the next command.
data.alternatives: Considered persistent shell sessions (PTY) but that's a much larger change. The pwd marker approach is simple and handles cd, pushd, popd, and any command that changes directories.

data.goal: Design implementation plan for replacing spawn-per-command terminal with PTY-based terminal using node-pty + xterm.js, with multi-tab support
Understand node-pty integration in Electron app for terminal UI and backend architecture

data.description: PTY terminal: Keep styled UI, wire node-pty as backend. Rejected xterm.js canvas rendering because it would make the terminal look like every other IDE. Path 1: persistent PTY for capabilities (cd, env persistence, Ctrl+C) while keeping Pane's unique styled input/output block UI.
data.why: User explicitly values Pane's terminal looking different from VS Code/iTerm. The styled prompt, separate input area, and command/output blocks are intentional design choices. xterm.js would override all of this.
data.alternatives: xterm.js canvas (full terminal), hybrid (styled UI + xterm fallback for interactive programs)
data.rejected_because: Interactive programs (vim, less, REPL) don't need to work in Pane's terminal — users have Terminal.app for that. The 95% use case is quick commands.

data.description: Implemented persistent PTY terminal with multi-tab support. Replaced spawn-per-command model with node-pty persistent shell sessions. Added TerminalTab store state, tab bar UI (only visible with >1 tab), Ctrl+C support, ANSI stripping, command completion markers, echo suppression, and 4 keyboard shortcuts for tab management.

data.goal: Audit all app initialization and background operations for scroll/performance blockers. Make Pane settle gracefully then run flawlessly.

data.description: Fixed persistent scroll jank in Pane caused by chokidar usePolling: true. Three issues: (1) chokidar polled filesystem every 1s across 5 directory levels — constant CPU and event storms even when idle, switched to native FSEvents (usePolling: false), (2) file-changed event payload mismatch — backend sent { paths: [...] } object but renderer expected string[] array, causing silent TypeError that broke the entire file watcher chain, (3) backend debounce was only 300ms, increased to 800ms.
data.root_cause: chokidar usePolling: true with interval: 1000 in main.mjs
data.secondary_bug: event payload was { paths: [...] } but renderer expected string[] — .filter() on object is TypeError
data.lesson: usePolling is for Linux inotify EMFILE concerns, not macOS FSEvents

data.description: Fixed EMFILE: too many open files from chokidar. Root cause: usePolling: false with depth: 5 and only dotfile ignore pattern meant chokidar tried to watch every file in node_modules/.next/dist etc. with native fs watchers, each consuming a file descriptor. Fix: expanded ignore list (node_modules, .next, dist, build, out, target, .turbo, coverage), reduced depth 5→3, added error handler.
data.why_polling_was_there: original dev hit EMFILE with native watchers because node_modules wasnt ignored — polling was the band-aid
data.real_fix: ignore heavy directories + reduce depth, then native FSEvents works fine

data.description: Root cause of non-smooth scrolling on ProMotion: Electron/Chromium defaults to 60fps frame rate cap. On a 120Hz display, every other frame was duplicated — explaining the "not natural" feeling even when the event loop was idle. Fixed with: (1) disable-frame-rate-limit Chromium flag to unlock 120Hz, (2) enable-gpu-rasterization for GPU compositing, (3) enable-zero-copy to reduce memory copies, (4) will-change: transform on all 4 scroll containers for compositor layer promotion.
data.irony: migrated from Tauri because WKWebView was 60fps-capped, but Electron was also 60fps by default

data.description: DO NOT add Chromium flags disable-frame-rate-limit, disable-gpu-vsync, enable-gpu-rasterization, or enable-zero-copy to Pane. These were tried on 2026-02-23 and DROPPED FPS TO 50. The remaining 120Hz scroll stutter is a known Chromium CVDisplayLink bug (Issue 40202100), not fixable at app level. Only fix is waiting for Chromium to migrate to CADisplayLink.

data.goal: Review what was done in previous sessions
Continuing work

data.description: Pane is now being used for real work - this Claude Code session is running inside Pane itself. User reports it's "quite refreshing"
data.significance: This is the validation moment - the tool transitioned from being built to being used for actual work
data.context: After 6 sessions of building/optimizing, Pane is now the daily driver

data.description: Fixed Claude streaming hang that caused app to freeze during summarization (when outputting ### headings). Two root causes: (1) JSON.parse of multi-MB messages on renderer main thread, (2) 20-50 Zustand store mutations/sec during text streaming.
data.fix_1: Parse Claude CLI JSON in main process, send pre-parsed objects via IPC structured clone — eliminates synchronous JSON.parse on renderer
data.fix_2: rAF-based text/thinking delta throttling — accumulate deltas in module-level strings, flush once per animation frame via requestAnimationFrame

data.description: Added strict TypeScript and ESLint enforcement to Pane. tsconfig.json with strict: true, noUnusedLocals, noUnusedParameters, noUncheckedIndexedAccess, noFallthroughCasesInSwitch. ESLint with prefer-const, no-var, typescript-eslint strict config. Added npm run typecheck and npm run lint scripts.
data.why: Code was correct by discipline alone — no tooling enforced immutability or strictness. Aligns with Aslam's identity: 'strict TypeScript, zero violations' and 'build with precision'.
data.files_fixed: MarkdownText.tsx (noUncheckedIndexedAccess), Terminal.tsx (same), App.tsx (same), projects.ts (same), useSettingsPersistence.ts (same), FileSearch.tsx (readonly ref), FileViewer.tsx (type mismatch), main.tsx (unused import), Settings.tsx (unused var), FileTree.tsx (unused var), GitStatus.tsx (unused param)
data.noUncheckedIndexedAccess: Most fixes were ! non-null assertions on bounds-checked array access. This is the right pattern — the bounds check guarantees the value, but TypeScript can't track it.

data.description: App freeze during Claude summarization: Main process blocks on JSON.parse + structured clone of multi-MB assistant messages. When Claude sends a final 'assistant' message containing the entire conversation summary (including file contents from tool results), it arrives as a single line that can be several megabytes. JSON.parse in main process and webContents.send structured clone both block the main process event loop, freezing the entire app.
data.pattern: Freeze happens at ## heading because that's where summarization output begins — but the actual block is from the preceding massive assistant/user messages, not the heading itself
data.root_cause: Electron main process is single-threaded. JSON.parse of multi-MB string + structured clone serialization of the parsed object both happen synchronously, blocking all window operations

data.description: Fixed Claude streaming hang/freeze during summarization. Root cause was main process blocking on JSON.parse() + structured clone of multi-MB assistant/user messages from Claude CLI. Fix: skip parsing lines >100KB that are assistant/user type, send lightweight stubs instead. Renderer handles stubs via "skipped" in msg check.
data.root_cause: JSON.parse of multi-MB lines + webContents.send structured clone blocks Electron main process event loop
data.strict_mode_fix: Changed 'as Record<string, unknown>' to '"skipped" in msg' for TypeScript strict compliance

data.goal: Research whether JSON.parse() and webContents.send() structured clone serialization can freeze Electron main process with large payloads

data.description: Comprehensive research on JSON.parse + webContents.send blocking Electron main process confirms the diagnosis is correct. JSON.parse of 50MB string takes 1.3 seconds per Node.js official docs. V8 structured clone adds further serialization cost. Event loop starvation from rapid readline callbacks compounds the problem. Solutions: skip large JSON.parse (current approach), use worker threads, use Electron utilityProcess, or batch/throttle IPC sends.
data.context: Researching whether JSON.parse + structured clone serialization in webContents.send can freeze Electron main process
data.key_numbers: JSON.parse(50MB) = 1.3s blocking, JSON.stringify(50MB) = 0.7s, IPC call overhead = 0.08ms, Node.js JSON.parse throughput ~85-140 MB/s, structured clone 2x faster than old base::Value but still blocking, flooding IPC queue degrades latency by orders of magnitude
data.conclusion: Diagnosis is 100% correct. Current >100KB skip optimization is sound.

data.description: Tool activity expand/collapse redesign: last 3 tool_use blocks stay expanded, older ones auto-collapse. User manual toggle always overrides. Logic moved from per-component useEffect to parent-driven forceExpanded prop computed from allToolUseIds.slice(-3).
data.why: Previous behavior: each tool expanded on mount, collapsed instantly when result arrived. Too fast to read — felt like flickering. New behavior gives a readable window of the 3 most recent tools.
data.alternatives: Considered timer-based delay (setTimeout before collapse), but that's fragile and doesn't respect the actual workflow. Parent-driven approach is declarative and reactive.

data.goal: Understand complete Claude CLI streaming pipeline in Electron app to plan UtilityProcess migration

data.description: Thoroughly explored entire Claude CLI streaming pipeline in Pane Electron app - analyzed main process handlers, preload script, renderer IPC bridge, consumer hooks, and build configuration

data.goal: Design migration of Claude CLI process management from Electron main process to UtilityProcess

data.description: Moved Claude CLI process management from Electron main process to UtilityProcess. Main process is now a zero-cost relay that never touches JSON.parse or Claude data. New file: src/main/claude-worker.mjs handles spawn, readline, JSON.parse in a separate V8 isolate. Removed the 100KB skip hack from both main.mjs and useClaude.ts.
data.why: JSON.parse of multi-MB Claude messages + structured clone in webContents.send blocked main process for 50-150ms+, freezing the entire app. The 100KB skip hack was band-aid treatment.
data.architecture: Main → UtilityProcess (postMessage relay) → Claude CLI (spawn/readline/JSON.parse). Worker is long-lived, pre-forked on app.whenReady(). Crash recovery sends synthetic processEnded to renderer.
data.files_changed: src/main/claude-worker.mjs (new), src/main/main.mjs, src/renderer/hooks/useClaude.ts, scripts/copy-compiled.js, scripts/dev.js, package.json

data.description: The Pane app crash is caused by node-pty, NOT Claude streaming. Both crash reports show identical stack: pty.node Napi::Error::ThrowAsJavaScriptException during node::Environment::CleanupHandles. node-pty tries to throw a JS exception from a ThreadSafeFunction callback during environment shutdown, which calls std::terminate -> abort(). This is a known node-pty bug with Electron.
data.misdiagnosis: We spent two sessions assuming Claude JSON.parse was the cause. The UtilityProcess migration was good architecture but did not fix this crash.

data.goal: Research node-pty SIGABRT crash in Electron during environment cleanup

data.description: node-pty SIGABRT crash in Electron is a known bug with specific fixes. The crash happens when node-pty's ThreadSafeFunction callback tries to throw a JS exception during environment teardown (napi_closing state). PR #672 (merged into v1.1.0) fixed the assertion failure by handling napi_closing status in SetupExitCallback. But the VS Code issue #243952 shows the IDENTICAL crash stack (ThrowAsJavaScriptException -> CallJS -> CleanupHandles) is STILL happening on v1.1.0+ with Electron 34+. The fix in PR #672 handles BlockingCall returning napi_closing, but the crash in #243952 happens inside the CallJS default callback itself when it tries to call a JS function on a torn-down environment. These are two different code paths.
data.workaround: Process isolation via UtilityProcess or child_process.fork

data.description: Fixed node-pty SIGABRT crash on app quit. Root cause: before-quit handler called pty.kill() without disposing event listeners first. node-pty's native exit callback thread would fire ThreadSafeFunction::CallJS during environment teardown, throwing an uncatchable C++ exception -> std::terminate -> SIGABRT. Fix: dispose dataDisposable and exitDisposable first, then kill via process.kill(pid, SIGKILL) to bypass node-pty native cleanup, then delay app.quit() by 100ms to let native threads settle.
data.long_term: Move node-pty into a UtilityProcess (same as VS Code's PTY Host pattern) to fully isolate the main process from node-pty crashes

data.description: Always read crash reports before guessing root causes. We spent two sessions assuming the freeze was Claude JSON.parse blocking the main process. The actual crash was node-pty SIGABRT during cleanup — completely unrelated. The crash logs (~/Library/Logs/DiagnosticReports/) had the answer the whole time: pty.node in the stack trace, not anything Claude-related.
data.context: macOS .ips crash reports contain full native stack traces. Parse them with python3 json module — first line is metadata, rest is JSON with threads[faultingThread].frames array.

data.goal: Move node-pty into its own UtilityProcess (PTY Host pattern) to fully isolate main process from node-pty crashes

data.description: Moved node-pty into its own UtilityProcess (pty-worker.mjs), mirroring the Claude worker pattern. Main process no longer imports or touches node-pty at all. If node-pty's known SIGABRT bug (vscode#243952) crashes the worker, only the PTY host dies — main process survives and sends synthetic exit events to renderer for crash recovery. Both Claude CLI and PTY now run in isolated UtilityProcesses, making the main process a pure relay with zero native crash surface.
data.pattern: VS Code PTY Host pattern — same as claude-worker.mjs
data.removed_from_main: node-pty require, activePtys Map, sendToRenderer$1, registerPtyHandlers body, __cjs_mod__ import
data.crash_recovery: ptyWorker.on('exit') sends synthetic pty-exit to all active PTY IDs
data.before_quit: Simplified to just postMessage({type:'shutdown'}) + kill() for both workers

data.goal: Research Electron utilityProcess.fork() and native module isolation — diagnosing why pty.node crashes on CrBrowserMain thread

data.description: Electron UtilityProcess is a truly separate OS process with its own PID and address space. Native .node addons loaded via require() in a UtilityProcess are loaded into that process's address space only — they cannot "leak" into the main process. Communication between main and utility processes is exclusively via Mojo IPC (serialized messages), with zero shared memory for native modules. If crash reports show pty.node on CrBrowserMain thread, the crash is either: (1) misattributed in the crash report (utility process thread 0 can sometimes be labeled CrBrowserMain in symbolication tools), or (2) there's an accidental import path pulling node-pty into the main process bundle. VS Code issue #243952 documents the exact same crash (pty.node Napi::ThreadSafeFunction::CallJS during node::Environment::CleanupHandles) and it occurs in the UTILITY process during shutdown, not the main process.
data.why: Diagnosing crash reports showing pty.node on CrBrowserMain. Electron PR #41277 fixed a NodeService destructor ordering bug that caused similar crashes. The fix was to ensure the NodeService message pipe is destroyed last during utility process teardown.
